lib.title: Every Story Needs a Hero!
lib.arch: Ruby/Amethyst Super midrange — evasive pressure, damage control, and big challenge turns
lib.tagline: Slippery magic buys time until the Supers hit back.
cards: 4 Dumbo - Ninth Wonder of the Universe
4 Demona - Scourge of the Wyvern Clan
4 Isis Vanderchill - Ice Queen of St. Canard
2 Peter Pan & Tinker Bell - Fast Friends
heroArts: Demona - Scourge of the Wyvern Clan
Peter Pan & Tinker Bell - Fast Friends
Dumbo - Ninth Wonder of the Universe
lib.strategy:
Ruby/Amethyst tempo-control deck built to win by developing a stable board, denying the opponent’s best quests/challenges, then converting Evasive characters into card draw and lore through Dumbo - Ninth Wonder of the Universe.

Main win plan: survive the early game, curve into efficient bodies, then stick Dumbo. Dumbo is the engine: he is Evasive, can exert/pay 1 to draw a card and gain 1 lore, and gives that same ability to other Evasive characters. Frozone - Super Slick, Violet Parr, and Mrs. Incredible become very important because they let the deck gain lore while drawing cards instead of running out of gas. Use Dumbo turns to snowball: every extra card drawn should either keep the board full, find Hades/Demona, or protect the final lore push.

Mulligan: prioritize a clean curve. Keep Luisa, Scrooge, Frozone - Super Slick, Junior Woodchuck Guidebook, Cheshire Cat, Violet, Dumbo, and one strong 4-cost play. Do not keep too many 5-6 cost cards unless the rest of the hand already plays smoothly. Junior Woodchuck Guidebook is excellent when the hand needs fuel, but do not spend tempo cracking it too early unless you are missing plays or need to refill.

Early game: play bodies, ink every turn, and avoid falling behind on board. Luisa and Bob Parr are early board presence. Scrooge McDuck - Ghostly Ebenezer can grow with Boost and become hard to trade into. Frozone - Super Slick is a premium early play because Evasive makes him a future Dumbo target. Against aggro, challenge only when it protects your lore race or prevents too much opponent lore. Against control, pressure early and force them to answer small threats before Dumbo arrives.

Mid game: shift from board building into board control. Cheshire Cat is one of the best tempo tools: Boost him to move damage from your characters onto opposing characters, turning damaged survivors into removal pressure. Isis Vanderchill and Frozone - Super Cool exert opposing characters so your board can quest safely or stop key opposing questers. Hades is your pressure valve: choose an opposing character and either the opponent bottoms it or you draw 2 cards. Both outcomes are good, so use Hades on characters the opponent really wants to keep.

Dumbo plan: once Dumbo is in play, every Evasive character becomes a mini-engine. If an Evasive character only quests for 1, using Dumbo’s granted ability is usually better because you still gain 1 lore and also draw a card. If Mrs. Incredible is gaining extra lore for the turn, decide between racing with questing or drawing with Dumbo based on whether you need cards or immediate lore. Do not expose Dumbo unnecessarily; he is the deck’s best way to beat control and long games.

Demona plan: Demona is both comeback tool and finisher. When played, she exerts all opposing characters and refills players with fewer than 3 cards up to 3. Use her when the opponent has a board that would otherwise race or challenge you, or when exerting everything lets you swing the game with safe quests. Because of Stone By Day, manage your hand count carefully; if you have 3 or more cards, Demona will not ready. She is strongest when you are low on cards, ready to refill, and ready to close the game.

Hercules plan: Hercules protects himself from damage unless challenged, and while exerted he protects your other Hero characters from non-challenge damage. Use him against decks that rely on damage effects instead of normal challenges. He is especially important for keeping your Super/Hero board alive while you set up Dumbo and Demona turns.

Closing the game: win by building a wide Evasive/Super board, drawing extra cards with Dumbo, then using Isis, Frozone - Super Cool, Hades, and Demona to remove or exert blockers. The final push often comes from a Demona turn that freezes the opposing board, followed by Evasive characters gaining lore safely while Dumbo keeps cards flowing.

Common mistakes to avoid: do not ink all your draw; do not play Dumbo into obvious removal without getting value; do not use every character only to challenge when you are supposed to be racing; do not overdraw with Demona if it stops her from readying next turn; do not forget that Dumbo’s granted ability can be better than normal questing on 1-lore Evasive characters.

Loss conditions: the deck loses when it misses early ink, falls too far behind before Dumbo, lets aggro quest freely, or holds too many expensive cards without stabilizing. It also loses when Dumbo is removed before producing value and the hand runs empty. The main discipline is to keep a playable curve, protect the board, and only switch into full control mode when it buys real lore advantage.

lib.title: Dale’s Growing Pressure!
lib.arch: Amber/Ruby Dale Pressure Midrange -  damaged-character value, high-willpower challenges, and relentless board pressure.
lib.tagline: Turn damage into value, then let Dale make every challenge unfair.
lib.strategy:
Amber/Ruby Dale Pressure deck built to win by creating early board presence, turning self-damaged characters into card advantage, then using Dale - Ready for His Shot to make high-willpower characters dominate challenges and clear the opponent’s board.

Main win plan: develop a board faster than the opponent, pressure lore every turn, and force bad challenges. The deck becomes much stronger once Dale is in play because your characters deal challenge damage with their willpower instead of their strength. This turns characters like Mulan - Elite Archer, Zeus - Defiant God, Scrooge McDuck - Ghostly Ebenezer, Chip - Retrieval Expert, Bambi - Ethereal Fawn, and even Dale himself into dangerous board-control pieces. Your opponent should feel like every challenge against you is bad.

Mulligan: look for a playable curve and at least one value engine. Keep Mulan - Injured Soldier, Reuben - Sandwich Expert, Grandmother Willow, Webby’s Diary, Dale, Chip, Scrooge, Bambi, Ohana Means Family, and This Growing Pressure. Do not keep too many expensive cards unless your early plays are already solved. Mulan - Injured Soldier plus Reuben or Ohana is one of the best starts because it turns her starting damage into tempo or cards.

Early game: play Mulan - Injured Soldier aggressively. She starts damaged, but that is not a weakness in this deck; it enables Reuben and Ohana. Reuben can remove damage and discount your next character, helping you double-spell or play above curve. Ohana Means Family can turn damaged characters into a big refill, especially on Zeus or Mulan. Grandmother Willow helps smooth awkward hands by making your next character cheaper.

Mid game: establish Dale and start controlling combat. Once Dale is active, challenge math changes completely. High-willpower characters become removal tools. Chip is especially important because he can return a character with 4 or more willpower from discard and also gives Dale +1 willpower, making Dale harder to remove and stronger in challenges. Protect Dale whenever possible because he is the card that makes the whole deck unfair.

Mulan plan: Mulan - Injured Soldier is both an early play and the shift target for Mulan - Elite Archer. Shift Mulan - Elite Archer when you need to swing board control. With Dale in play, Mulan can deal damage based on her willpower during challenges, which makes her Triple Shot effect much more threatening. Without Dale, Medallion Weights can still boost her strength and let her challenge while drawing cards. Do not waste Mulan into a small target unless the extra damage clears important opposing characters.

Damage package: Zeus - Defiant God and Mulan - Injured Soldier enter damaged, which sets up Ohana Means Family and Reuben. Ohana is your biggest comeback and refill card: use it when you can remove multiple damage and draw multiple cards, not just to heal 1. Reuben is tempo: use him to heal and immediately reduce the next character you play. The ideal turn is heal damage, draw or discount, then add another strong body.

Boost package: Scrooge McDuck, Bambi, and Gaston all care about cards being placed under characters. Webby’s Diary rewards this plan by letting you pay 1 to draw whenever a card goes under one of your characters or locations. Bambi can become a major draw engine after Boosting because whenever he exerts during your turn, he reveals cards based on the number of cards under him and adds character cards to your hand. Scrooge grows bigger with each card under him and becomes especially dangerous with Dale because his willpower rises too.

Pressure tools: This Growing Pressure and Gaston force opposing characters into awkward lines by stopping them from challenging and making them quest if able. Use these effects on the opponent’s best challenger, bodyguard, or high-lore threat. The goal is to make them expose characters so your Dale-powered board can clean them up.

Removal tools: The Horseman Strikes! is your clean answer to Evasive characters and replaces itself by drawing a card. Medallion Weights turns a key challenge into card draw and can help Mulan or another attacker clear larger targets. Raging Storm is your panic button. Do not fire it just because you are slightly behind; use it when the opponent has committed more board than you, when you can rebuild faster, or when you have Chip/Ohana/Bambi/Webby’s Diary ready to recover.

Closing the game: after you gain board control, stop over-challenging and start questing. This deck can accidentally play too much control and forget to win. Dale helps you win challenges, but the goal is still 20 lore. Once the opponent’s board is weak, use Gaston and This Growing Pressure to stop their best comeback challenge, then quest with your wide board. If they rebuild, punish them with Mulan, Medallion Weights, or Raging Storm.

Common mistakes to avoid: do not ink all your Ohana copies; do not play Dale without a plan to protect or use him; do not waste Ohana for only 1 card unless desperate; do not forget Webby’s Diary triggers when you Boost; do not use Raging Storm when you are the one ahead; do not tunnel on challenges when you can simply race to 20 lore.

Loss conditions: the deck loses when it misses early ink, draws too many expensive cards without Reuben/Willow support, loses Dale before getting combat value, or wastes its healing/draw too early. It also struggles if the opponent removes your board before Ohana or Bambi can refill. The discipline is to curve out, turn damage into advantage, protect Dale, and only shift into full control when it creates a clear lore advantage.

lib.title: Boostie Boys
lib.arch: Location-control midrange — use removal and tempo tools to slow the opponent, build value through Scrooge’s Counting House, then close with big threats like Maleficent, Sulley & Boo, Hercules, and repeated board pressure.
lib.tagline: Freeze the board, build the bank, finish with monsters.
lib.strategy:
This version is more proactive and location-focused than the previous list. You are not relying as much on healing/value from Ohana Means Family or Agustin, so you need to be cleaner with your removal and board sequencing.

Early game, look for Webby’s Diary, Scrooge McDuck, Liquidator - Iced Over, Raging Storm, and Scrooge’s Counting House. Your goal is to stop fast decks from snowballing while setting up a value engine.

Midgame, the deck wants to control the pace with Aladdin - Barreling Through, The Horseman Strikes!, Red Alert, Gaston - Superior Archer, and Liquidator. You are trying to make the opponent’s characters awkward: damaged, frozen, removed, or forced into bad trades.

Your main pressure package is Scrooge’s Counting House + Scrooge McDuck + Sulley/Boo package. Besties, Assemble!, Boo - Energetic Child, and Sulley & Boo - Scare Buddies suggest the deck wants a stronger character-synergy/value plan instead of pure removal control.

Late game, Maleficent - Monstrous Dragon is your reset/finisher. After you clear the opponent’s best threat, you pivot into lore with your surviving board and locations.

Main weakness: this list may be less forgiving because it has less healing and fewer recovery tools. If you fall behind too early, you need your removal to be precise. But if you stabilize, this version should close better with stronger board pressure.
